explanation_md: `char` variables printed with `cout` display the character whose ASCII code matches the stored integer, not the raw number. ASCII code 65 is the character `'A'`. To see the number 65 you would need to cast `c` to an `int` first.
Both variables are initialised with their given values and printed with a space between them and a newline at the end. There is no arithmetic here; the values come out exactly as stored.

prompt_md: Which of the following best describes why C++ requires every variable to have a type?

options.a: So the programmer must write more code, making programs harder to misuse.
options.b: So the compiler knows how much memory to reserve and how to interpret the stored bits.
options.c: So that the runtime can change the type when a different value is assigned.
options.d: Types are optional; they are only needed when using floating-point numbers.

explanation_md: The type tells the compiler exactly two things: how many bytes to allocate and what the bit pattern means (integer, floating-point, character, etc.). Option c describes dynamic typing (like Python), not C++. Option d is wrong — all variables in C++ require a type.

prompt_md: A programmer writes `short s = 40000;`. What is the most likely result on a platform where `short` is 2 bytes?

options.a: The program prints 40000 because C++ promotes short to int automatically.
options.b: The compiler refuses to compile the program.
options.c: The value is stored as-is; the platform silently upgrades the short to fit.
options.d: The value wraps around and s holds an unexpected (often negative) number.

explanation_md: A 2-byte signed `short` can only hold values from -32768 to 32767. Assigning 40000 causes integer overflow: the bit pattern wraps and the result is an unexpected value, typically negative. The compiler may warn but will not error. Brace initialisation (`short s{40000}`) would be a compile error instead.

prompt_md: Which initialisation syntax will cause a compile error when the value does not fit the type?

options.d: None of the above — all three behave identically.

explanation_md: Brace initialisation (`{}`) disallows narrowing conversions and produces a compile error if the initialiser does not fit the target type. The other two syntaxes silently truncate the `double` to `3` without any diagnostic.
